  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This report is a false positive for CWE-079. The reported source is a request header used by `Jenkins.getRootUrlFromRequest()` to reconstruct a URL, but the reported sink is only `EnvVars.put`, which stores that URL into the `HUDSON_URL`/`JENKINS_URL` environment variables for process-launch/build context. The path is also conditional: `Jenkins.getRootUrl()` only uses request-derived headers when the global root URL is unset and there is an active Stapler request; otherwise it returns the configured URL or `null`. None of these operations produce HTML/JS output or reflect attacker input into a web page, which is required for CWE-079. At most, this is an environment-variable propagation path, not XSS.",

  "response": "{

  "analysis": "The reported dataflow is real, but it does not represent CWE-079. `doUploadPluginImpl` reads a user-controlled multipart field (`items.get(1).getString()`), passes it into `new UrlPluginCopier(fileName)`, and `UrlPluginCopier.copy` fetches bytes from `new URL(url)` via `ProxyConfiguration.getInputStream(...)`. That means the tool found a reachable user-input-to-network-fetch path, not an input-to-HTML/JavaScript/browser-output path. For CWE-079, the sink must place attacker-controlled data into a web page or script-execution context; this code only downloads a plugin archive to disk. The path is also part of an intentional Jenkins feature: admin users can deploy a plugin from a URL. The endpoint is `@RequirePOST`, requires `Jenkins.ADMINISTER`, and Jenkins enforces a crumb for POST requests. So this report is a false positive for the claimed CWE.",

  ],
  "evidence": "Relevant path: `core/src/main/java/hudson/PluginManager.java:1945-1956` checks `Jenkins.get().checkPermission(Jenkins.ADMINISTER);`, parses the multipart request, then does `String string = items.get(1).getString();` and if nonblank sets `copier = new UrlPluginCopier(fileName);`. In `core/src/main/java/hudson/PluginManager.java:1902-1905`, `UrlPluginCopier.copy` executes `try (InputStream input = ProxyConfiguration.getInputStream(new URL(url))) { Files.copy(input, target.toPath()); }`. The UI confirms this is intended behavior: `core/src/main/resources/hudson/PluginManager/advanced.jelly:49-60` contains a form for `uploadPlugin` with textbox `name=\"pluginUrl\"`, and `core/src/main/resources/hudson/PluginManager/advanced.properties:23-25` explicitly says admins can 'provide a URL to install a plugin from outside the configured update site(s)'. POST requests are CSRF-protected by crumb validation in `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/csrf/CrumbFilter.java:116-160`.",

No untrusted data is written into an HTTP response, a Jelly page, DOM, script block, or other browser-executable context. In addition, the code comment explicitly states this path is intended for the instance creator, meaning the practical control is with the deployer/admin rather than a remote attacker. Therefore, even if the header reaches `createAnnotator`, that alone does not trigger XSS.   "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported dataflow is real, but it is not a real CWE-079 issue and is not attacker-controllable in the normal Jenkins threat model. The sink is an outbound download/openConnection path, not an HTML/JavaScript rendering sink, so it does not match CWE-079 at all. For the SSRF-like interpretation in the report explanation, the supposed source is still wrong: `core.url` comes from signed update-center metadata or from an update-site URL that only an administrator can configure. So this is not an A1/A2 reachability problem; the path is reachable, but the source/sink trust model is wrong.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection"
  ],
  "evidence": "`UpdateSite.Entry` resolves the download URL from metadata: `String url = o.getString(\"url\"); if (!URI.create(url).isAbsolute()) { url = URI.create(baseURL).resolve(url).toString(); } this.url = url;` in `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/UpdateSite.java:783-790`. That value reaches the download sink in `HudsonUpgradeJob.getURL()` via `return new URL(site.getData().core.url);` in `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/UpdateCenter.java:2725-2729`, then `connect(job, src)` calls `job.site.connect(src)` / `ProxyConfiguration.open(src)` and finally `url.openConnection()` in `UpdateCenter.java:1334-1434`, `UpdateSite.java:213-215`, and `ProxyConfiguration.java:311-319`.\n\nHowever, this URL is not ordinary user input. Before `data = new Data(o)` is accepted, `UpdateSite.updateData` performs signature verification when `signatureCheck` is enabled: `if (signatureCheck) { FormValidation e = verifySignatureInternal(o); if (e.kind != FormValidation.Kind.OK) return e; } ... data = new Data(o);` in `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/UpdateSite.java:243-270`. `DownloadService.signatureCheck` is enabled by default (`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DownloadService.java:505-509`), and `JSONSignatureValidator.verifySignature` validates the certificate chain (`core/src/main/java/jenkins/util/JSONSignatureValidator.java:56-89`).\n\nChanging the update-site URL itself is also privileged: `PluginManager.doSiteConfigure` requires `Jenkins.ADMINISTER` before executing `sites.add(new UpdateSite(UpdateCenter.ID_DEFAULT, site));` in `core/src/main/java/hudson/PluginManager.java:1845-1851`. So an external or low-privilege attacker cannot realistically steer this URL. If an administrator deliberately points Jenkins at a malicious update site or disables signature checking, that is trusted-admin configuration/misconfiguration, not a Jenkins vulnerability. Also, nothing in this path emits untrusted data into a web page; it is a download path, and the core upgrade flow even verifies checksums before rewriting `jenkins.war` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/UpdateCenter.java:2748-2753`).",

}"
}

In `BasicAuthenticationFilter#doFilter`, Jenkins builds `path = req.getContextPath() + \"/secured\" + req.getServletPath()` and appends the query string only after `?`, then uses that value as the `Location` header for an internal authentication redirect. Because the tainted data is only in the query component of a same-origin redirect target, it does not become executable script or HTML in the browser.   "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "FP. The reported path does not produce a real CWE-079 vulnerability, and it is not a real open redirect either. The report conflates CWE-079 with open redirect behavior, but even under an open-redirect interpretation the flow is not exploitable. `Authentication.getName()` is not inserted into HTML/JS and is not used as raw redirect control data. It is only stored inside `ResourceDomainRootAction.Token`, then transformed by `Token.encode()` into an opaque URL-safe Base64 value over `HMAC || UTF-8(timestamp:length:username:path)`. The redirect target is then built as a validated resource-domain base URL plus `static-files/` plus that encoded token plus URL-encoded path components. So the username never reaches `sendRedirect` in a dangerous form. The destination host is also not attacker-controlled; it is the admin-configured resource root URL, which is validated by `ResourceDomainConfiguration`. This is therefore a false positive caused primarily by missing the sanitizing/opaque-token transformation in the path.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "`core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupport.java:396-398` calls `rsp.sendRedirect(302, ResourceDomainRootAction.get().getRedirectUrl(resourceToken, req.getRestOfPath()))`. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRootAction.java:158-164` constructs the redirect as `resourceRootUrl + getUrlName() + \"/\" + token.encode() + Arrays.stream(restOfPath.split(\"[/]\")).map(Util::rawEncode).collect(Collectors.joining(\"/\"))`. Thus the path part is URL-encoded via `Util.rawEncode`, and the username does not flow directly into the URL. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRootAction.java:289-296` shows the crucial transformation: `String value = timestamp.toEpochMilli() + \":\" + username.length() + \":\" + username + \":\" + path; ... return Base64.getUrlEncoder().encodeToString(result);` after prepending `KEY.mac(valueBytes)`. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRootAction.java:299-306` verifies the MAC on decode, so attackers cannot tamper with the token to alter the redirect semantics. `core/src/main/java/hudson/Util.java:919-920` documents that `rawEncode` encodes a single URL path component. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainConfiguration.java:107-130` validates the resource root URL and rejects using the same host as Jenkins, so the host part is a trusted admin configuration, not user input.   "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported dataflow is reachable, so this is not mainly an A1/A2 issue. `newName` does come from an HTTP request in `Job.doDoRename`, flows through rename handling, and reaches `Job.SubItemBuildsLocationImpl.onLocationChanged`. However, the reported sink is only a server-side log write (`LOGGER.severe(String.format(..., newFullName, oldFullName))`), not an HTML response, DOM write, script context, or any other browser-executable sink. That means the report does not demonstrate CWE-079 at all; the decisive error is incorrect sink selection (B1). The code also applies validation before rename: `AbstractItem.doCheckNewName` calls `Jenkins.checkGoodName(newName)`, which rejects ISO control characters and dangerous characters including `<` and `>`. So even the secondary idea of log forging is not realistically exploitable through this path. Finally, when Jenkins displays log records in the UI, the relevant Jelly views are escape-by-default and render messages with `j:out`, so viewing the logs does not create XSS either.",

    "B1"

  "evidence": "Source: `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Job.java:1654-1655` reads `newName` from the HTTP request and passes it to `doConfirmRename`. Validation: `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/AbstractItem.java:287-296` calls `Jenkins.checkGoodName(newName)` before `renameTo(newName)`. Sanitizer: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:4311-4325` rejects ISO control characters and the characters `?*/\\\\%!@#$^&|<>[]:;`. Reachable path: `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/AbstractItem.java:451` calls `ItemListener.fireLocationChange(this, oldFullName)`, and `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/listeners/ItemListener.java:249-258` computes `newFullName` and notifies listeners. Reported sink: `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Job.java:740-743` only inserts `newFullName` and `oldFullName` into `LOGGER.severe(String.format(...))`. Log rendering is escaped in the UI: `core/src/main/resources/hudson/logging/LogRecorder/index.jelly:28` declares `escape-by-default='true'`, and `core/src/main/resources/lib/hudson/logRecords.jelly:36-38` outputs the message with `<j:out value=\"${parts[3]}\"/>`. Access to log pages is also restricted, per `core/src/main/java/hudson/logging/LogRecorder.java:95-97`.",

The source `request.getReferer()` is user-influenced, but the reported sink `new HttpRedirect(redirect)` does not create a real CWE-079 condition here, and it does not even create a real open redirect on this path. In `ReverseProxySetupMonitor.doTest`, the redirect target is always built from `j.getRootUrl()` (a Jenkins-controlled base URL) plus a fixed internal path, and the tainted `referer` is only appended as one encoded path component via `Util.rawEncode(...)`. `Util.rawEncode` encodes `/`, `:`, `?`, `#`, `%`, `<`, `>`, and other unsafe URI characters, so the referer cannot break out of that path component or replace the destination host/scheme. The follow-up handler `getTestForReverseProxySetup` merely compares the decoded path token against `j.getRootUrlFromRequest() + \"manage\"` and returns `200` or a `404` diagnostic message; this is a reverse-proxy self-test endpoint, not an HTML/JavaScript execution sink. Therefore the reported path does not lead to exploitable XSS in the real world, and it also does not let an attacker redirect a victim to an arbitrary external site. The report additionally mixes CWE-079 with open-redirect reasoning, which is another sign of misclassification, but the primary issue is the sink selection.",

  ],
  "evidence": "Relevant code: `ReverseProxySetupMonitor.doTest` builds `redirect = j.getRootUrl() + ... + \"testForReverseProxySetup/\" + (referer != null ? Util.rawEncode(referer) : \"NO-REFERER\") + \"/\";` and then returns `new HttpRedirect(redirect)` (core/src/main/java/hudson/diagnosis/ReverseProxySetupMonitor.java:77-94). The user-controlled value is not used as the redirect destination; it is embedded under a trusted Jenkins root URL. Sanitization: `Util.rawEncode` is documented to encode a single path component and explicitly encodes slash and HTTP special characters such as `/ ; : ?` plus unsafe chars like `# % < > ...` (core/src/main/java/hudson/Util.java:910-920, 938-950). Result: a referer like `https://evil.example/x?<script>` becomes percent-encoded and the browser is redirected only to a Jenkins URL such as `https://jenkins.example/administrativeMonitor/.../https%3A%2F%2Fevil.example%2Fx%3F%3Cscript%3E/`, not to `evil.example`. Follow-up logic: `getTestForReverseProxySetup(String rest)` only checks `if (rest.startsWith(inferred))` then returns OK, else returns a 404 diagnostic (core/src/main/java/hudson/diagnosis/ReverseProxySetupMonitor.java:100-108). Intended use is confirmed by the client code and tests: `resources.js` calls this endpoint via `fetch` as a reverse-proxy diagnostic, and `ReverseProxySetupMonitorTest` sets a `Referer` like `<jenkins-url>manage` to validate this behavior (core/src/main/resources/hudson/diagnosis/ReverseProxySetupMonitor/resources.js:41-60; test/src/test/java/hudson/diagnosis/ReverseProxySetupMonitorTest.java:46-120).",

The reported path goes through `toStemUrl()`, which returns only a URL string. That value is rendered into escaped HTML attributes and, on the client side, is consumed by the modern validation branch that appends query parameters and calls `fetch(...)`. No script is executed, no HTML is injected, and no browser XSS primitive is reached. The source is also overstated: `currentDescriptorByNameUrl` comes from a server-set request attribute or Stapler ancestor URL, not a raw attacker-controlled request parameter. The main error is B1 (incorrect sink/source selection), with a secondary D1 aspect because the analysis ignored the `checkDependsOn` gate that prevents the legacy `eval(checkUrl)` behavior.",

    "B1"
  ],

  "analysis": "This report is a false positive for CWE-079. The reported source may be user-influenced in the narrow sense that `archive.getName()` comes from a plugin archive filename, but the selected sink is not an XSS sink. In `ClassicPluginStrategy.createPluginWrapper`, the filename is used only to derive a local extraction directory: `expandDir = new File(..., getBaseName(archive.getName()))` at `core/src/main/java/hudson/ClassicPluginStrategy.java:164`. Then `explode(...)` creates `destDir/.timestamp2` and calls `new FilePath(explodeTime).touch(archive.lastModified())` at `ClassicPluginStrategy.java:472-488`. `FilePath.touch()` performs local filesystem operations only; it does not render data into HTML, JavaScript, or an HTTP response. The later `FilePath.isDescendant(...)` lines cited by the report are also not XSS sinks; they are server-side path validation and logging. So even if the filename were attacker-controlled, the reported source/sink pair cannot produce browser-executed script. Separately, the path construction is also unsound: `touch()` calls `act(new Touch(timestamp))`, but the cited lines 3755-3814 belong to the different callable `IsDescendant.invoke(...)`, so that part of the path is not a real runtime flow.",

(4) `core/src/main/java/hudson/FilePath.java:1208-1210`: `act(...)` invokes the specific callable on `new File(remote)`. For `touch()`, that callable is `Touch`, not `IsDescendant`. The reported sink, `blacklist.add(withoutExclamation)`, is not an HTML/JavaScript/HTTP response sink; it merely stores a signature string for later policy checks. That data is later consumed by `decide()` to return `ACCEPTED`, `REJECTED`, or `UNKNOWN`, and then by `DoActionFilter` / `TypedFilter` to allow or deny Stapler routing. No file content is rendered into a browser page, embedded into script, or otherwise used in a way that could execute attacker-controlled script. The path itself is reachable, but it only implements routing-policy configuration, not output generation. In `parseFileIntoList` the dataflow is: `if (line.startsWith(\"!\")) { String withoutExclamation = line.substring(1); if (!withoutExclamation.isEmpty()) { blacklist.add(withoutExclamation); } } else { whitelist.add(line); }` (StaticRoutingDecisionProvider.java:252-263). The stored values are then only checked by `decide()`: blacklist hit => `Decision.REJECTED`; whitelist hit => `Decision.ACCEPTED`; otherwise `Decision.UNKNOWN` (StaticRoutingDecisionProvider.java:87-105). `DoActionFilter.keep()` uses that decision to return `true` or `false` for routing (DoActionFilter.java:67-79), and `TypedFilter.keep()` uses the same decision for fields/functions and return types (TypedFilter.java:137-160, 194-215). The reported sink location is backend bookkeeping only: `Nodes.handleAddedNode` calls `node.onLoad(this, node.getNodeName())` and, on error, `nodes.compute(node.getNodeName(), ...)` inside `Queue.withLock(...)` (Nodes.java:189-203; Queue.java:1280-1287). Explaination: The source is the username obtained from Jenkins.getAuthentication2().getName(), which is user-controlled data. This data flows through the system without any apparent sanitization and is used in a context where it could be rendered in a web interface, potentially leading to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) if the data includes malicious scripts. The sink is where the data is added to a causeBag, which could be displayed to users, making it a valid XSS sink.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. Step 1: the source may be attacker-influenced enough to warrant review, but the sink is misidentified. `CauseAction.causeBag.put/compute` is only internal storage, not HTML output, so it is not an XSS sink (B1). Step 2: the reported path is a real storage path, but it stops before any browser-rendering sink, so the vulnerability is not triggerable as reported. Jenkins renders build causes through Jelly views with `escape-by-default='true'`, and `Cause.getShortDescription()` is explicitly treated as text in this codebase. So even if the value is later displayed, the normal rendering path does not execute it as HTML/JS. This is primarily B1, with secondary missed sanitization/view-semantics issues rather than A2 or C1.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Source/storage only: `core/src/main/java/hudson/cli/BuildCommand.java:167` creates `new CauseAction(new CLICause(Jenkins.getAuthentication2().getName()))`; `CLICause.getShortDescription()` is at `BuildCommand.java:252-256`; `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/CauseAction.java:58-64` only stores the cause in `causeBag`. Real UI rendering is separate: `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/CauseAction/summary.jelly:29-34` renders each cause via `<st:include page=\"description.jelly\" it=\"${entry.key}\"/>`. Jenkins explicitly documents at `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Cause.java:71-74` that `getShortDescription()` is interpreted as text, not HTML. The generic cause view `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/Cause/description.jelly:24-26` has `escape-by-default='true'` and outputs `${it.shortDescription}`, which escapes attacker-controlled content. The `UserIdCause` view `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/Cause/UserIdCause/description.jelly:24-31` is also `escape-by-default='true'`. Additionally, `CLICause` extends `UserIdCause` but only sets its own `startedBy` field (`BuildCommand.java:248-256`) and does not initialize `userId`; in the standard cause-view path this means the inherited `UserIdCause` template takes the anonymous-user branch rather than exposing `startedBy` as raw HTML. Jenkins also has an explicit opt-out for i18n argument escaping via `Functions.rawHtml` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/Functions.java:2386-2389`), and that opt-out is not used here.",

{
  "report": "Vulnerability Path:
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/Permission.java:220-220: getName(...) : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/Permission.java:220-220: ... + ... : String
core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:68-68: getId(...)

Explaination: The source 'owner.getName() + '.' + name' potentially concatenates user-controlled data without validation, which can be tainted. The sink 'rsp.addHeader' directly uses this data in HTTP headers, which could lead to HTTP Header Injection if the data includes malicious characters like CR and LF. However, without explicit evidence of user control over 'owner.getName()' or 'name', it's uncertain if this is a direct vulnerability.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported path is reachable, but the source is not attacker-controlled. `AccessDeniedException3.reportAsHeadersImpl` writes `permission.getId()` into the `X-Required-Permission` response header, yet `permission` comes from Jenkins' internal authorization logic, not from request data. `Permission.name` is rejected unless it is a valid Java identifier, and `getId()` is built from `owner.getName() + '.' + name`, where `owner` is a `Class` from `PermissionGroup`. These are code-defined permission metadata values and cannot carry the CR/LF characters needed for header injection. So this path is not exploitable in practice. Separately, the report is also misaligned with CWE-079 because the sink is response-header output rather than HTML/JavaScript rendering, but the primary false-positive cause is incorrect source identification.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Dataflow: `ACL.checkPermission` throws `new AccessDeniedException3(a, p)` with an existing internal `Permission p` when authorization fails (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/ACL.java:71-80`). The sink is `rsp.addHeader(\"X-Required-Permission\", permission.getId())` in `core/src/main/java/hudson/security/AccessDeniedException3.java:59-70`. The `Permission` constructor enforces `if (!JSONUtils.isJavaIdentifier(name)) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)` and then sets `this.id = owner.getName() + '.' + name;` (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/Permission.java:142-155`). The fallback path in `getId()` returns the same concatenation (`core/src/main/java/hudson/security/Permission.java:218-221`). Example permissions in the codebase are static constants such as `new Permission(PERMISSIONS, \"Read\", ...)` and `new Permission(PERMISSIONS, \"Delete\", ...)`, confirming the header value is framework permission metadata, not remote user input.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

Explaination: The source 'req.getSubmittedForm()' retrieves data directly from a user request, which is typically untrusted. The data flows through the application without any explicit sanitization and is used to create a new Node object. This Node object is then added to a collection in the system, potentially influencing system behavior or data integrity. The sink 'nodes.put(node.getNodeName(), node)' involves storing the Node object, which includes user-controlled data, without sufficient validation or encoding, potentially leading to unsafe data handling.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The input source is user-controlled, but the reported sink is not an XSS sink. The path from `req.getSubmittedForm()` to `NodeDescriptor.newInstance(...)` to `app.addNode(...)` only creates and registers a `Node`; the final operation `nodes.put(node.getNodeName(), node)` stores the object in an internal map. CWE-079 requires attacker-controlled data to reach an HTML/JavaScript/browser DOM output context without proper encoding. This report never shows such a rendering sink. In addition, the node name used as the map key is validated before creation and dangerous characters such as `<` and `>` are rejected, and normal Jenkins Jelly views render node names with escaping enabled. So this is not a real XSS issue in the reported path.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection"
  ],
  "evidence": "`ComputerSet.doDoCreateItem` first enforces authorization with `app.checkPermission(Computer.CREATE)`, then validates the name via `checkName(fixedName)`, then binds the submitted form into a `Node` and calls `app.addNode(result)`. `checkName` calls `Jenkins.checkGoodName(name)`, and `Jenkins.checkGoodName` rejects unsafe characters including `<` and `>`. The alleged sink in `jenkins/model/Nodes.java` is only `Node old = nodes.put(node.getNodeName(), node);`, which is a `ConcurrentMap` insertion and not browser output. The standard UI pages that display node names use `<?jelly escape-by-default='true'?>`; for example, `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/ComputerSet/index.jelly` renders `${c.displayName}` inside an escaped Jelly page, and `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/Computer/index.jelly` similarly uses `${it.displayName}` with escape-by-default. `hudson/model/Node.java` also builds URLs with `Util.rawEncode(getNodeName())`. Therefore the report stops at persistence/state update and does not demonstrate any executable HTML/JS sink.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "FP. The primary issue is B1: the reported sink is not an XSS sink. `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/UserExperimentalFlagsProperty.java:77-86` only reads submitted JSON and stores non-empty strings into a `Map<String,String>`. That operation does not generate HTML, JavaScript, or an HTTP response, so even if the input is attacker-controlled, CWE-079 is not triggered there. In the real core code path, stored flag values are later read via `UserExperimentalFlag.getFlagValue(User)` and converted to the expected type. For the only built-in flag in this tree, `NewBuildPageUserExperimentalFlag`, the type is boolean; `BooleanUserExperimentalFlag.deserializeValue(...)` accepts only `\"true\"` or `\"false\"`, and any payload like `<script>...</script>` becomes `null`, causing a fallback to the default value. The related Jelly views are also `escape-by-default='true'`, and the config UI does not print the raw stored value. The report path is additionally imprecise because it starts from `AbstractProject.buildDescribable(...)`, while this user-property descriptor is actually reached from `UserPropertyCategoryAction.doConfigSubmit(...)`, not from project trigger/configuration building.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "1. Reported sink is only storage, not rendering: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/UserExperimentalFlagsProperty.java:77-86` -> `JSONObject flagsObj = formData.getJSONObject(\"flags\"); ... String value = (String) flagsObj.get(key); if (!value.isEmpty()) { flags.put(key, value); }`.\n2. Actual use of stored data enforces type conversion: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/UserExperimentalFlag.java:91-107` gets the stored object and calls `deserializeValue(value)`; if conversion returns null, it returns the default value.\n3. Only built-in implementation is boolean: `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/NewBuildPageUserExperimentalFlag.java:34-48` extends `BooleanUserExperimentalFlag`, and `core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/BooleanUserExperimentalFlag.java:50-58` accepts only `\"true\"`/`\"false\"`. So a payload such as `<script>alert(1)</script>` is discarded semantically and never becomes active UI content.\n4. No raw echo in the UI: `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/UserExperimentalFlagsProperty/config.jelly:25-66` is `escape-by-default='true'`; it only fetches `flagValue` and includes the flag config/description pages. `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/BooleanUserExperimentalFlag/flagConfig.jelly:25-37` uses `flagValue` only in boolean comparisons for `<f:option selected=...>` and never outputs the raw string. `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/model/experimentalflags/UserExperimentalFlag/flagDescription.jelly:24-26` uses `<j:out value=\"${it.shortDescription}\" />`, which is escaped and is not derived from user-submitted flag values.\n5. The reported path start is not the real dispatcher: `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/AbstractProject.java:1889-1901` builds `Describable` instances for project configuration (`d.newInstance(req, data.getJSONObject(safeName))`), while this code is actually reached from `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/userproperty/UserPropertyCategoryAction.java:33-50`, which calls `d.newInstance(req, o)` for user properties. So the constructed path is also not a concrete project-to-user-property execution path.\n6. Real-world exploitability is further reduced because the submit endpoint is admin-gated: `UserPropertyCategoryAction.doConfigSubmit` calls `this.targetUser.checkPermission(Jenkins.ADMINISTER);` before reading the form.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

Explaination: The source 'req.getServerName()' can potentially be tainted if manipulated by an attacker through HTTP headers, specifically the 'X-Forwarded-Host'. The sink involves a redirection URL which is constructed using this potentially tainted host value. If an attacker can control the host header, they can manipulate the redirection URL leading to an open redirect vulnerability, which is a common issue in web applications.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "1. Source/sink check: the source is plausibly attacker-influenced because `Jenkins.getRootUrlFromRequest()` trusts `X-Forwarded-Host` / `getServerName()` when Jenkins root URL is not configured. But the reported sink is `new HttpRedirect(redirect)`, which generates an HTTP redirect response, not HTML or JavaScript output. That is not a CWE-079 sink. 2. Path check: the path is real and reachable through the administrative monitor endpoint, so this is not mainly an A1/A2 false positive. 3. Runtime semantics: this endpoint is only a reverse-proxy self-test; the browser-side code calls it with `fetch` and only inspects the HTTP status to decide whether to show a warning. The attacker-controlled host value is never rendered into a page or script context. 4. CWE alignment: the report explanation itself describes an open redirect / host-header manipulation scenario, which deviates from CWE-079. Therefore the report is a false positive for CWE-079.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection"],
  "evidence": "`Jenkins.getRootUrl()` first uses the configured root URL if present (`Jenkins.java:2460-2464`), and only falls back to request-derived data when not configured (`Jenkins.java:2465-2467`). `getRootUrlFromRequest()` constructs `scheme://host[:port]/context/` from `X-Forwarded-Proto`, `X-Forwarded-Host`, or `req.getServerName()` (`Jenkins.java:2514-2551`). `ReverseProxySetupMonitor.doTest()` then builds `redirect` and returns `new HttpRedirect(redirect)` (`ReverseProxySetupMonitor.java:76-95`), i.e. a redirect response, not HTML rendering. Access to this monitor is gated by `AdministrativeMonitor.getTarget()` calling `checkRequiredPermission()` (`AdministrativeMonitor.java:257-261`), and this monitor requires `Jenkins.SYSTEM_READ` (`ReverseProxySetupMonitor.java:113-115`). On the client side, `ReverseProxySetupMonitor/resources.js` sends a `fetch` to the `/test` endpoint and only checks `response.status` / failure to decide whether to display the warning (`resources.js:18-27`, `41-61`). No attacker-controlled value is injected into HTML/JS. Also, the only other request-derived component included in the redirect path, `referer`, is encoded with `Util.rawEncode(...)` (`ReverseProxySetupMonitor.java:89-91`, `Util.java:919-920`). If there is any issue here at all, it is closer to a limited open-redirect/host-header concern on a diagnostic endpoint, not XSS.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported flow is real, but it is not a real CWE-079 vulnerability in Jenkins' normal threat model. `DirectoryBrowserSupport` does read a value and place it into CSP response headers, but the value is not derived from attacker-controlled request data. It comes only from a fixed configuration key, `hudson.model.DirectoryBrowserSupport.CSP`, via `SystemProperties.getString(...)`, which first checks JVM system properties and then `ServletContext.getInitParameter(...)`. Those are deployment-time/admin-controlled configuration channels, not untrusted web input. The path is therefore reachable, but the chosen source is not a valid XSS source.\n\nThis is also an intentional configuration override point, not an accidental unsanitized flow. Jenkins ships with a restrictive default CSP for directory browsing, and explicitly allows administrators to override or disable it. Jenkins even warns administrators that overriding this property is a potential security issue when browsing untrusted files. So the report identifies a configurable security-control knob, not a vulnerability where an attacker can inject script-bearing content through this dataflow. At most, a privileged administrator could deliberately weaken CSP and thereby make browsing untrusted artifacts less safe, but that is an insecure configuration choice, not a product XSS flaw.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Relevant code shows the value is configuration-driven and fixed-key, not request-driven:\n- `core/src/main/resources/META-INF/web-fragment.xml:170-173` registers `jenkins.util.SystemProperties$Listener`, which initializes access to servlet context init parameters.\n- `core/src/main/java/jenkins/util/SystemProperties.java:105-110` sets `handler = key -> theContext.getInitParameter(key);`.\n- `core/src/main/java/jenkins/util/SystemProperties.java:244-249` returns `handler.getString(key)` only after checking `System.getProperty(key)`.\n- `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupport.java:107` defines the fixed key `CSP_PROPERTY_NAME = DirectoryBrowserSupport.class.getName() + \".CSP\"`.\n- `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupport.java:402-406` reads `String csp = SystemProperties.getString(CSP_PROPERTY_NAME, DEFAULT_CSP_VALUE);` and then sets CSP headers with that value.\n- `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupport.java:877` shows the secure default CSP value.\n- `core/src/main/java/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRecommendation.java:42-44,60-63` explicitly describes this property as a system-property override of the default CSP and activates an admin warning when it is set.\n- `core/src/main/resources/jenkins/security/ResourceDomainRecommendation/message.properties:1-2` says overriding `hudson.model.DirectoryBrowserSupport.CSP` is a potential security issue when browsing untrusted files, confirming it is an admin-controlled escape hatch.\n- `test/src/test/java/hudson/model/DirectoryBrowserSupportTest.java:330-338` intentionally sets the system property to an empty string and verifies the headers are omitted, proving this behavior is designed.\n\nSo the reported path exists, but the source is trusted deployment configuration, not attacker input; no real attacker-controlled XSS flow is demonstrated.",

The sink 'url.openConnection().getInputStream()' opens a connection and retrieves data from the URL without any validation or sanitization of the URL, which could lead to security vulnerabilities such as Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) if the URL is maliciously crafted. However, the specific vulnerability mentioned (Cross-Site Scripting, CWE-079) is not directly applicable here as XSS primarily affects web page contexts where HTML or JavaScript is executed in the user's browser. The actual vulnerability risk in this scenario is more aligned with SSRF or similar URL-based attacks.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This report is a false positive for CWE-079 (XSS). The reported source is real in the sense that `httpCon.getHeaderField(\"Location\")` in `FilePath.installIfNecessaryFrom` can read attacker-controlled redirect data from a remote HTTP server, and the flow is reachable because the method explicitly follows redirects. However, the reported sink is not an XSS sink. The value is turned into a `URL` (`getUrlFactory().newURL(location)`) and later consumed by backend download code (`ProxyConfiguration.getInputStream(archive)` / `url.openConnection().getInputStream()`) to fetch archive bytes for tool installation. That is network I/O, not HTML/JavaScript generation, browser rendering, or any response-writing path. So even if the dataflow exists, it does not trigger CWE-079. At most, this pattern is closer to SSRF / unsafe remote download concerns, not XSS. The path itself is not obviously unreachable: Jenkins has a unit test `installIfNecessaryFollowsRedirects` showing this redirect-following logic is intended behavior. So the main issue is not A1/A2. The key failure is incorrect source/sink selection for the target CWE definition. There is also no nearby sanitizer issue needed to explain the FP: the path never reaches a web output sink. Even the only nearby textual use of `location` is a log line (`Following redirect ... -> ...`), and Jenkins console HTML rendering escapes plain text before display, so that does not become XSS either.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"

  "evidence": "Relevant code in `core/src/main/java/hudson/FilePath.java` shows: `String location = httpCon.getHeaderField(\"Location\");` then `return installIfNecessaryFrom(getUrlFactory().newURL(location), listener, message, maxRedirects - 1);`. The `UrlFactory` implementation is just `return new URL(location);` (`FilePath.java:3352-3353`). Later the archive is consumed as `InputStream in = archive.getProtocol().startsWith(\"http\") ? ProxyConfiguration.getInputStream(archive) : con.getInputStream();` (`FilePath.java:1039`). In `core/src/main/java/hudson/ProxyConfiguration.java`, `getInputStream(URL url)` is implemented as `((HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection(...)).getInputStream();` (`ProxyConfiguration.java:342-348`). This is purely server-side HTTP fetching, not browser output. Reachability is confirmed by the unit test `installIfNecessaryFollowsRedirects` in `core/src/test/java/hudson/FilePathTest.java`, which mocks a 302 response, supplies a `Location` header, and verifies installation continues through the redirected URL. Also, the only nearby rendering use is logging: `listener.getLogger().println(\"Following redirect \" + archive.toExternalForm() + \" -> \" + location);` (`FilePath.java:990`). Jenkins console HTML rendering escapes plain text via `MarkupText.toString(true)` -> `Util.xmlEscape(text)` / `Util.escape(text)`, so even that log output is not an XSS sink.",

No part of the reported path renders the attacker-controlled data into a web page, script block, DOM, or any browser-executable context, so CWE-079 cannot be triggered. The path is not false because of unreachable control flow; it is false because the chosen sink does not correspond to XSS.
